== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]] [[Category:moved from a redlink]]

== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]]

== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]]

== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]]

== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]]

== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]]

== TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 14101711 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5270005002349 == Commons category (P373) == * Oi-jinja (Tottori) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県八頭郡若桜町屋堂羅1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oi-jinja (Tottori)}} [[JA:意非神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11493043}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oi Shrine |Image = [[File:Oi-jinja01s4592.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Established = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Oi-no-miya (former name) |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923|1=October 9}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tottori|label=Oi Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oi Shrine''' (おいじんじゃ, いひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}}. The enshrined deity is Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto, and the shrine crest is the crossed-arrow motif. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Overview === This shrine was established by descendants of Ame-no-Nigihayahi-no-Mikoto to enshrine their ancestral deity. It is an ancient shrine with over a thousand years of history, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. It stands on a hill at the entrance to the Yadora district, beyond the {{ill|lt=Hattō River|Q11391449|WD=Q11391449|1=Hatto river}} from {{ill|lt=Wakasa-juku|Q11616547|WD=Q11616547|1=Wakasa-juku}}. In ancient times it was called “'''Oi-no-miya'''” and was located in Ichinomiya Valley (Fukada), but according to tradition it was relocated to Yaotoshi Valley (= Yadora = Yadora), the place where an arrow shot from the shrine grounds by {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} is said to have landed. At the foot of the hill stands Japan’s largest {{ill|lt=nobori banner|Q1068772|WD=Q1068772|1=Nobori}}. === Shrine grounds === ; Japan’s largest nobori banner It was erected on October 8 of the first year of the Heisei era as a reconstruction of Oi Shrine’s “five-tan nobori,” which existed until around {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}} (Shōwa 20). A Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (100 years old, 31 m tall) is used as the pole. The banner measures 26.8 m in height and 1.8 m in width, equivalent in area to 30 tatami mats. The calligraphy was done by {{ill|lt=Shigenobu Sakano|Q11425611|WD=Q11425611|1=Shigenobu Sakano}} for the banner, {{ill|lt=Yuji Nishio|Q11627787|WD=Q11627787|1=Yūji Nishio}} for the hoisting platform, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kenji Takemoto|1=UNKNOWN}} for the commemorative monument. ; One of Japan’s largest wooden torii gates Erected on January 30 of Heisei 23 to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the accession of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=UNKNOWN}}. As with the banner pole, it was built using Yadora-grown {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} (over 80 years old, milled from trees 18 m tall). Its dimensions are 10.8 m in height and 9.2 m in width, ranking it among the top five wooden torii gates in Japan (as of May, Heisei 23). The shrine name plaque calligraphy was done by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Norie Nagano|1=UNKNOWN}}, head of the Tottori Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. The initiator and overall person in charge was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayuki Iino|1=UNKNOWN}}, head parish representative of Oi Shrine. File:Oi-jinja02s4592.jpg|Main hall File:Oi-jinja04s4592.jpg|Japan’s largest nobori banner === On-site information === ;Location * {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|WD=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yazu District|Q1043163|WD=Q1043163|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Wakasa Town|Q1156116|WD=Q1156116|1=Wakasa, Tottori}} Yadora 1 ;Access * Nearest station: Get off at {{ill|lt=Wakasa Station|Q7960835|WD=Q7960835|1=Wakasa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Wakasa Railway|Q11616555|WD=Q11616555|1=Wakasa Railway}}, then a 7-minute walk ;Nearby * {{ill|lt=Wakasa Local History and Culture Village|Q11616556|WD=Q11616556|1=Wakasa Town Historical Village}} * {{ill|lt=Nakanoshima Park (Wakasa Town)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nakanoshima Park}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Wakasa (Tottori)}} {{Shinto portal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oi Shrine (Tottori)]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:鳥取県の旧村社]] [[Category:若桜町の建築物]] [[Category:若桜町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]]
